1: veahavta et YHWH eloheicha (wĕʾāhabtā ʾet Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā): 'Love the LORD your God.' ʾāhab (love) is the covenant-loyalty term that integrates both emotion and action — it is not merely affective feeling but the orientation of the whole self toward the covenant partner. mišmartô (his requirements/charge): derived from šāmar (to keep, guard), denoting the whole range of covenant obligations — statutes (ḥuqqôt), ordinances (mišpāṭîm), and commandments (miṣwōt). The four-fold enumeration signals the comprehensiveness of covenant obligation.
2: yedatem hayom (yĕdaʿtem hayyôm): 'Know today.' The address shifts to the living generation — the children have not known (yādaʿ, the experiential-relational knowing) the exodus events. mûsar YHWH (the discipline/instruction of YHWH): mûsār denotes formative correction through experience — the hard lessons of the wilderness. The knowing that grounds obligation is grounded in witnessed history, not mere tradition.
3: otot umaasav (ʾōtōtāyw ûmaʿaśāyw): 'His signs and deeds.' ʾôt (sign) denotes a confirmatory marker; maʿaśeh (deed/work) the accomplished act. Together they describe the pattern of the exodus: sign and wonder pair as YHWH's credentials in Deut 4:34; 7:19; 26:8. Egypt is the theater of divine demonstration.
4: asher hetzif et-mei yam-suph (ʾăšer hēṣîp̄ ʾet-mê yam-sûp̄): 'Who caused the waters of the Reed Sea to overflow/engulf.' yam sûp̄: traditionally rendered Red Sea, but sûp̄ means reed — the Reed Sea or Sea of Reeds. hēṣîp̄ from ṣûp̄ (to flow over, overwhelm): the specific verb used in Ps 124:4-5 of the waters that 'would have engulfed us.' The drowning of the Egyptian army is the definitive act of holy war.
5: mah-asah lachem bamidbar (mâ-ʿāśâ lākem bammidbar): 'What he did to you in the wilderness.' The wilderness (midbar) is the place of YHWH's sustained provision and discipline — manna, water from the rock, quail — and the site of Israel's repeated failure. Both the provision and the punishment are part of the covenantal knowing that the current generation possesses.
6: asher asah leDatan velaAviram: The Dathan and Abiram episode (Num 16) — the earth swallowed them. wattiphtaḥ hāʾădāmâ ʾet-pîhā (the ground opened its mouth): the reversal of the Genesis creation in which Adam was formed from the ʾădāmâ — the earth that gave life takes it back as judgment. The covenant community is defined by remembering both the beneficence and the severity of YHWH.
7: kol-maaseh YHWH hagadol (kol-maʿăśēh Yhwh haggādôl): 'All the great work of YHWH.' haggādôl (the great): the emphatic divine epithet. The generation addressed has with their own eyes (ʿênêkem rōʾôt) seen all these great works — their eyewitness obligation is greater than their children's. Deuteronomy's pedagogy is grounded in the irreducible weight of direct experience.
8: ushmartem et-kol-hamitzvah (ûšĕmartem ʾet-kol-hammiṣwâ): 'Keep all the commandment.' mīṣwâ is singular despite the plural context — 'the commandment' as a comprehensive whole, the unified Torah-demand. The motivation: 'that you may be strong and go in and possess the land.' The indicative of redemption precedes the imperative of obedience (ch 1-11's recitation of YHWH's acts grounds the obligations of ch 12-26).
9: lehaʾarik yamim (lĕhaʾărîk yāmîm): 'To prolong your days.' ʾārak (to be long, to extend): the length-of-days formula that runs through Deuteronomy as the covenant-blessing consequence of obedience (5:16; 6:2; 17:20; 22:7; 25:15). The land flowing with milk and honey (ʾereṣ zābat ḥālāb ûdĕbaš): the classic double phrase for Canaan's fertility; zābat (flowing, dripping) from zûb (to flow) marks the land's natural abundance.
10: lo chemereṣ mitsrayim hi (lōʾ kĕʾereṣ miṣrayim hîʾ): 'Not like the land of Egypt.' The contrast is between Egypt's irrigated agriculture (dependent on the Nile, controlled by human labor: you watered it 'with your foot') and Canaan's rain-dependent agriculture (dependent entirely on YHWH's provision). The agricultural difference encodes a theological difference — Canaan requires faith in YHWH's rainfall; Egypt produces the illusion of human self-sufficiency.
11: ereẓ harim uveqaot (ʾereṣ hārîm ûbĕqāʿôt): 'A land of mountains and valleys.' The topography of Canaan — varied highland terrain requiring rain — contrasts with Egypt's flat flood-plain. lematar (for rain): the land 'drinks water from the rain of heaven' (šōtâ mayim lĕmaṭar haššāmayim). YHWH's eyes are upon it continuously — ʿênê Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā bāh (the eyes of YHWH your God are upon it) — making the land YHWH's ongoing concern and direct provision.
12: mereshit hashana (mērēšît haššānâ): 'From the beginning of the year.' The temporal expression marks YHWH's oversight as year-round — not occasional or seasonal but continuous surveillance. aḥărît šānâ (end of the year): the complete calendar under divine attention. The land is unlike Egypt's Nile-dependent cycle — Israel's agricultural success depends on YHWH's covenantal faithfulness expressed through seasonal rainfall.
13: veHAYA im-shamoʿa tishmeʿu (wĕhāyâ ʾim-šāmōaʿ tišmĕʿû): 'If you carefully obey.' The double infinitive absolute construction (šāmōaʿ tišmĕʿû) intensifies the obedience: 'if you listen-listening' = diligent, thoroughgoing attention. This formula introduces the covenant blessing-condition that parallels the Shema (6:4-9); obedience to love-and-serve (leʾāhăbâ... ûlĕʿobdô) earns seasonal rain.
14: yoreh umalkosh (yōreh ûmalqôš): 'Early rain and late rain.' The agricultural calendar of the Levant depends on two rain seasons: the yōreh (early rain, October-November, softening the ground for planting) and the malqôš (late rain, March-April, filling the grain before harvest). Both are promised as covenant blessings — YHWH controls the cycles on which Israel's survival depends. Drought is the default covenant-curse (28:22-24).
15: venatati esev besadcha livehemtecha: 'I will give grass in your fields for your livestock.' The abundance cascades from grain to grass to livestock to human eating — each element dependent on YHWH's rainfall provision. veʾākaltā veśābaʿtā (you will eat and be satisfied): śābaʿ (to be satisfied, sated) is the covenant-blessing fullness — not mere survival but abundance. Deut 8:10-12 warns against forgetting YHWH precisely when śābaʿ is achieved.
16: hishamevu lachem pen-yiphteh levavchem (hišāmĕrû lākem pen-yipteh lĕbabkem): 'Watch yourselves lest your heart be deceived.' pātâ (to be deceived, enticed, opened wide): the same root as in 13:6 (the enticer). The heart-deception leads to turning aside to serve other gods — sārtem waʿăbadtem ʾĕlōhîm ʾăḥērîm. The self-warning (hišāmĕrû lākem) is the reflexive imperative: guard yourselves; the danger is internal spiritual seduction, not only external pressure.
17: vehara af YHWH bachem (wĕḥārâ ʾap̄ Yhwh bākem): 'YHWH's anger will burn against you.' ḥārâ ʾap̄ (the nose/nostril burned): the idiom for fierce divine anger — the nose as the organ of breath and emotion in Hebrew physiology. The covenant curse sequence follows: no rain, ground bears no produce, exile from the good land. The agricultural withdrawal is the visible sign of broken covenant — YHWH reverses the blessings of vv.13-15.
18: vesamtem et-devarai eleh al-levavchem (wĕśamtem ʾet-dĕbārāy ʾēlleh ʿal-lĕbabkem): 'Place these words of mine on your hearts.' śûm ʿal-lēb (to set upon the heart): the Hebrew idiom for internalization, taking something to heart. The subsequent actions — binding on the hand, between the eyes, teaching to children, writing on doorposts (mĕzûzôt) — externalize what is first internal. tôṭāp̄ôt (frontlets/phylacteries): the symbol between the eyes; later worn as physical tĕp̄illîn in Jewish practice. The placement sequence (heart → hand → forehead → doorpost → gate) moves from the most internal to the most public.
19: velimadtem otam et-beneihem (wĕlimmdtem ʾōtām ʾet-bĕnêkem): 'Teach them to your children.' limmēd (to teach, train): the piel intensive of lāmad (to learn). The Torah's transmission is communal and generational — the command-love of 6:6-9 is repeated here in identical language, emphasizing the pedagogical responsibility of parents. dabbēr bām (speak them): in every life-setting — sitting, walking, lying down, rising up — Torah saturates daily existence.
20: ukhtavtam al-mezuzot beitecha (ûkĕtabtām ʿal-mĕzûzôt bêtekā): 'Write them on the doorposts of your house.' mĕzûzâ (doorpost): the vertical wooden post at a door's side; the practice of affixing a small scroll (mĕzûzâ in later usage) to doorposts derives from this command. The gate (šaʿar) extends the Torah-marking to public space. The household and its public entry point are both claimed by the covenant word.
21: leyan yirbu yemeichem (lĕmaʿan yirbû yĕmêkem): 'That your days may be multiplied.' The lĕmaʿan (in order that) teleological formula — the covenant's purpose is life and length of days in the promised land. yimê haššāmayim ʿal-hāʾāreṣ (the days of heaven upon the earth): a beautiful hyperbole for indefinite duration — as long as sky is above earth. The land-tenure formula pairs with obedience; disobedience forfeits the promise.
22: ki im-shamor tishmeroon (kî ʾim-šāmōr tišmĕrûn): 'If you carefully observe.' Again the double infinitive absolute intensifier (šāmōr tišmĕrûn). dābaq (to hold fast, cleave): the covenantal-adhesion term — used of husband and wife in Gen 2:24, of Ruth and Naomi in Ruth 1:14, and here of Israel's obligation to YHWH. dābaq is the most intimate of the covenant-loyalty verbs, denoting personal attachment rather than mere compliance.
23: vehorish YHWH et-kol-hagoyim: 'YHWH will drive out all the nations.' yāraš (to dispossess, take possession): the key land-theology verb — both the dispossessing of the Canaanites and the possessing by Israel are expressed by the same root. The nations are described as 'greater and mightier than you' — the dispossession is explicitly beyond Israel's own military capacity, requiring divine action.
24: kol-hamakom asher tidroch kaf-raglechem bo lachem yihyeh: 'Every place where you set the sole of your foot shall be yours.' The land grant is expressed in the bodily imagery of foot-on-ground — the physical act of walking the territory constitutes possession in ancient Near Eastern legal custom. The promised boundaries: wilderness to Lebanon, Euphrates to Western Sea (hayyām hāʾaḥărôn, the Mediterranean).
25: pachdam vemoraam (paḥdĕkem ûmôrāʾăkem): 'Your terror and dread.' paḥad (terror, dread) and môrāʾ (fear, reverential dread): the two fear-words denote the existential dread YHWH will place in the nations' hearts at Israel's approach. This reverses the fear-formula: Israel need not fear (v.25a: 'no man shall stand against you') because YHWH instills fear in the nations. The holy-war theology of Deut is grounded in divine action, not Israelite strength.
26: reeh anochi noten lifneichem hayom (rĕʾēh ʾānōkî nōtēn lip̄nêkem hayyôm): 'See, I am placing before you today.' rĕʾēh (see!) — the singular imperative of perception, calling each individual to direct moral attention. berākâ ûqĕlālâ (blessing and curse): the covenant's binary structure that governs Deut 28-30. The 'today' (hayyôm) is the existential now of covenant decision — the choice is always present and urgent.
27: et-haberacha asher tishmeʿu: 'The blessing, if you obey.' The blessing is conditional on šāmaʿ (hearing/obeying) — 'if you obey the commandments of YHWH your God that I am commanding you today.' The Hebrew conditional structure: ʾăšer tišmĕʿû... hammitsvâ... ʾăšer ʾānōkî mĕṣawweh ʾetkem hayyôm — the blessing is inextricably bound to obedience.
28: vehakelala im-lo tishmeʿu: 'The curse, if you do not obey.' The curse (qĕlālâ) is the consequence of not-hearing — lōʾ tišmĕʿû. sārtem min-hadderek (turning aside from the way): sûr (to turn aside, deviate) is the standard verb for apostasy in Deuteronomy — leaving the covenant path. The 'other gods' you have not known: ʾĕlōhîm ʾăḥērîm ʾăšer lōʾ-yĕdaʿtem — the gods without covenant history with Israel.
29: venatata et-haberacha al-har gerizim: 'Set the blessing on Mount Gerizim, the curse on Mount Ebal.' The two mountains of the Shechem valley — the geography of covenant ratification (Josh 8:30-35 enacts Deut 27-28). Gerizim (traditionally the blessing-mountain, to the south; the Samaritan temple stood here) and ʿEbal (the curse-mountain, to the north). The landscape itself becomes the covenant's visible declaration.
30: halo-hema beeiver haYarden: 'Are they not beyond the Jordan?' The geographical orientation — 'beyond the Jordan, toward the setting sun, in the land of the Canaanites in the Arabah, opposite Gilgal, beside the oaks of Moreh' — grounds the covenant ceremony in a specific, visitable landscape. The precision of the geographical description serves the covenant's historical particularity: this is not mythology but history attached to known places.
31: ki atem ovrim et-haYarden: 'For you are crossing the Jordan.' The crossing (ʿābar) is the liminal moment — the covenant people on the threshold of the promised land. The entire book of Deuteronomy is Moses's final address before the crossing; the covenant renewal here is the preparation for what follows in Joshua. The imperative to obey 'all the statutes and ordinances' applies to the land they are entering to possess.
32: ushmartem laasot et-kol-hachuqqim: 'Keep and do all the statutes and ordinances.' The closing verse of the chapter pairs šāmar (keep, guard) with ʿāśâ (do, perform) — both verbs required: internalized guarding AND external performance. The land they are about to enter demands both dimensions of covenant loyalty.
